1: A structural note: 'In the twelfth year in the twelfth month on the first day of the month the word of YHWH came to me.' March 585 BCE — fifteen months after Jerusalem's fall (July 586 BCE). The lament for Pharaoh begins after the destruction of Jerusalem is complete, suggesting that the Egypt-laments are the epilogue to Israel's judgment: YHWH's program of judgment has passed through his own people and now reaches their would-be savior. God judges his house first (1 Peter 4:17).
2: A type: 'Son of man, raise a lamentation over Pharaoh king of Egypt and say to him: You consider yourself a lion of the nations, but you are like a dragon in the seas; you burst forth in your rivers, trouble the waters with your feet, and foul their streams.' Pharaoh compares himself to a lion (kefir goyim) but YHWH compares him to a sea dragon (tannin bayayamim). The self-image and the divine diagnosis differ: Pharaoh sees royal majesty; YHWH sees primordial chaos. The dragon from the sea reappears in Revelation 13:1. Every human system that claims lion-majesty while being sea-dragon in its actual nature is Egypt-Pharaoh in new form. Christ alone is the true Lion (Revelation 5:5: “the Lion of the tribe of Judah has conquered”) who is not secretly a dragon.
3: A pattern: 'Thus says the Lord GOD: I will throw my net over you with a host of many peoples and they will haul you up in my dragnet.' YHWH's net spread over the sea dragon — the sovereign fisher. Jesus's call of the disciples (Matthew 4:19: “I will make you fishers of men”) recasts the fishing imagery in reverse: what was the net of judgment becomes the net of salvation. The same sovereign power that casts the net of judgment over the proud casts the gospel-net over the lost.
4: A structural note: 'And I will cast you on the ground; on the open field I will fling you and will cause all the birds of the heavens to settle on you and I will gorge the beasts of the whole earth with you.' Cast on the ground — the dragon of the sea brought to land and left exposed. The sea dragon out of its element: powerless, devoured. The same image of birds feasting on the fallen appears in Revelation 19:17-18. Every manifestation of the beast-from-the-sea power ends in this same exposure and defeat.
5: A structural note: 'I will lay your flesh on the mountains and fill the valleys with your height.' The immensity of Egypt's fall — even its body fills mountains and valleys. The scale of the image corresponds to the scale of Egypt's former greatness: the empire that spread over continents is now reduced to a corpse that fills the landscape. Greatness in death becomes grotesque. The resurrection of Christ is the counter-image: the body glorified rather than scattered, raised rather than spread across mountains.
6: A structural note: 'I will drench the land even to the mountains with your flowing blood and the watercourses will be full of you.' Egypt's blood flooding the Nile: the water-to-blood motif from the Exodus plagues (Exodus 7:17-21) is inverted. Egypt's own blood fills the rivers that Pharaoh claimed as his own (Ezekiel 29:3). The river of death for Egypt's blood flows along the same path where the river of life will one day flow (Ezekiel 47:1-12; Revelation 22:1).
7: A direct revelation: 'When I blot you out I will cover the heavens and make their stars dark; I will cover the sun with a cloud and the moon shall not give its light.' The darkening of the heavens at Egypt's fall is cosmic de-creation imagery: the celestial order worshipped in the solar cults of Heliopolis and Memphis goes dark. Jesus applies the same cosmic language to the parousia (Matthew 24:29: “the sun will be darkened and the moon will not give its light and the stars will fall”). Every prophetic darkening of sky is a type of the last day when Christ appears and the old order gives way.
8: A direct revelation: 'All the bright lights of heaven will I make dark over you and put darkness on your land, declares the Lord GOD.' “Put darkness on your land” — the ninth plague of Egypt (Exodus 10:21-23) comes full circle: the darkness that YHWH put on Egypt during the Exodus is replicated in Egypt's final judgment. And the darkness at the cross (Matthew 27:45: “from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land”) stands in the middle: the darkness at Golgotha is simultaneously a Passover-darkness (the true Passover lamb dying) and an Egypt-judgment darkness (the dragon finally judged). Christ on the cross is both the dying Lamb and the judged dragon-bearer.
9: A pattern: 'I will trouble the hearts of many peoples when I bring your destruction among the nations into the countries that you have not known.' The nations “troubled in heart” at Egypt's fall — the shock waves of imperial collapse reaching the edges of the known world. This disturbance among nations is one of the signs of the approaching Day of YHWH (Joel 3:9-16; Haggai 2:7: “I will shake all nations”). The final shaking of the nations (Hebrews 12:26-27) accompanies the coming of the one who “cannot be shaken” — Christ and his unshakeable kingdom.
10: A structural note: 'I will make many peoples appalled at you and the hair of their kings shall bristle with horror because of you when I brandish my sword before them. They shall tremble every moment, every one for his own life, on the day of your downfall.' Every king appalled — the solidarity of rulers watching Egypt fall. What could happen to the greatest empire can happen to any. The existential dread (“every one for his own life”) is what awaits every power that has not submitted to Christ. The fear of the Lord that is the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 9:10) is far better received before the judgment than in it.
11: A structural note: 'For thus says the Lord GOD: The sword of the king of Babylon shall come upon you.' Babylon as the sword of YHWH against Egypt — the same instrument YHWH used against Jerusalem. The Babylonian sword does not discriminate between YHWH's people and their enemies; both are cut by it in their appropriate season. The equal application of the divine instrument to both Israel and Egypt signals that YHWH's justice is impartial: “judgment begins at the household of God” (1 Peter 4:17) but does not stop there.
12: A structural note: 'By the swords of mighty ones I will cause your multitude to fall; they are all the most ruthless of nations. They shall bring to nothing the pride of Egypt and all its multitude shall perish.' The pride of Egypt (gaon Mitsraim) brought to nothing — pride specifically named as the target of YHWH's judgment. This is consistent throughout Ezekiel's oracle-against-nations section (Ezekiel 28:2, 5, 17 for Tyre; 31:10 for the cedar): the sin that makes YHWH judge is always the pride that mistakes the creature for the Creator.
13: A structural note: 'I will destroy all its beasts from beside many waters; no foot of man shall trouble them anymore nor hoof of beast trouble them.' The silencing of Egypt — not just the people but the whole animal life of the land ceases. The agricultural and commercial ecosystem collapses. The absolute desolation signals cosmic un-creation, the reversal of the inhabiting of the land. Only the new creation (Rev 21-22) corrects this: where Egypt's land is emptied, the new earth is filled with life.
14: A structural note: 'Then I will let the waters of Egypt run clear and cause their rivers to run like oil, declares the Lord GOD.' After all feet have ceased the waters of Egypt run clear — the land returns to a pristine state through desolation. The clarity of waters through abandonment is a shadow of the water-of-life clarity in the new creation (Revelation 22:1: “clear as crystal”). But Egypt's clarity is the emptiness of judgment; the new creation's clarity is the fullness of life.
15: A direct revelation: 'When I make the land of Egypt desolate and when the land is stripped of all that fills it, when I strike down all who dwell in it, then they will know that I am YHWH.' YHWH is known even in absolute emptiness — perhaps most clearly there, because all that previously crowded the vision of YHWH (the idols, the Pharaoh, the Nile, the empire) has been removed. The spiritual principle: when everything else is stripped away, YHWH is seen clearly. Paul learned this: “I have learned, in whatever state I am, to be content” (Philippians 4:11) — stripping away every worldly support revealed Christ as all-sufficient.
16: A structural note: 'This is a lamentation that shall be chanted; the daughters of the nations shall chant it; over Egypt and all her multitude shall they chant it, declares the Lord GOD.' The lament is formalized as a text to be performed — the daughters of the nations as professional mourners chanting Ezekiel's oracle. The lament over Egypt is given literary permanence: it becomes part of the prophetic canon that all subsequent readers engage. The permanent record of divine judgment against the proud is embedded in Scripture for all time.
17: A structural note: 'In the twelfth year in the fifteenth day of the month the word of YHWH came to me.' March 585 BCE — two weeks after the first lament of chapter 32. The second lament over Egypt is addressed to Pharaoh's descent to Sheol. The double lament (Ezekiel 32:1-16 and Ezekiel 32:17-32) is Ezekiel's most sustained meditation on death and the netherworld, surpassed in contrast only by the dry-bones vision (Ezekiel 37:1-14).
18: A structural note: 'Son of man, wail over the multitude of Egypt and send them down, her and the daughters of majestic nations, to the world below, to those who have gone down to the pit.' The prophetic cry enacts the judgment it announces — the word of YHWH is performative, accomplishing what it declares (Isaiah 55:11). The same performative word that sends Egypt to the pit is the word that calls Lazarus from the tomb (John 11:43) and will call all the dead at the last trumpet (1 Corinthians 15:52).
19: A structural note: '“Whom do you surpass in beauty? Go down and be laid to rest with the uncircumcised.”' The mocking question — “whom do you surpass in beauty?” — echoes the cedar oracle's “to which of the trees of Eden are you to be compared?” (Ezekiel 31:18). The answer is the same: nothing now. Go down. The descent to the pit is YHWH's final verdict on every claim to superior beauty and power. Christ's descent into the realm of the dead (1 Peter 3:18-20; Acts 2:27-28) is the total inversion: he goes down not under verdict but in victory, not to remain but to rise.
20: A pattern: 'They shall fall amid those who are slain by the sword. Egypt is delivered to the sword; drag her away and all her multitudes.' Fall among the slain — the great empire joins the nameless dead. The sword was Egypt's instrument of power; now it is Egypt's instrument of death. “Those who live by the sword will die by the sword” (Matthew 26:52) — Egypt is the historical illustration of the principle Jesus names explicitly. The kingdom that advanced by the sword is ended by the sword.
21: A direct revelation: 'The mighty chiefs shall speak of them, with their helpers, out of the midst of Sheol: “They have come down, they lie still, the uncircumcised, slain by the sword.”' The mighty chiefs of Sheol speaking — the gathered dead of all nations recognizing Egypt's arrival. This is the “Sheol-taunt” form that appears in Isaiah 14 over Babylon: “How you are fallen from heaven, O Lucifer!” (Isaiah 14:12). When Christ arrives in the realm of the dead, it is as the herald of liberation, not as a fellow captive joining the assembly (1 Peter 3:18-20).
22: A structural note: 'Assyria is there and all her company, its graves all around it, all of them slain, fallen by the sword.' The roll call of nations in Sheol begins with Assyria — the great empire that preceded Babylon. Assyria had the same cedar-greatness (Ezekiel 31:3) and the same Sheol-destination. The vision of Sheol as populated by the fallen empires of history gives eschatological depth to the judgment. Daniel's vision of all the nations before the Ancient of Days (Daniel 7:9-14) is the judicial counterpart to Ezekiel's vision of the nations in Sheol.
23: A structural note: 'Whose graves are set in the innermost parts of the pit and her company is all around her grave; all of them slain, fallen by the sword, who spread terror in the land of the living.' The innermost parts of the pit — the depths of Sheol — for those who spread the most terror. The measure of terror spread in life corresponds to the depth of descent in death. The eschatological reversal: in the new creation, those who were most humble in life are most exalted (Matthew 5:3-11).
24: A structural note: 'Elam is there and all her multitude around her grave; all of them slain, fallen by the sword, who went down uncircumcised into the world below, who spread their terror in the land of the living; and they bear their shame with those who go down to the pit.' “They bear their shame”: shame persists in Sheol. The consciousness of shame in the realm of the dead is the OT's glimpse of what the NT calls eternal condemnation. Shame separated from Christ's covering (Revelation 3:18: “white garments so that the shame of your nakedness may not be seen”) is permanent.
25: A structural note: 'They have made her a bed among the slain with all her multitude, her graves all around it, all of them uncircumcised, slain by the sword; for terror of them was spread in the land of the living, and they bear their shame with those who go down to the pit; they are placed among the slain.' The repeated phrase “bear their shame with those who go down to the pit” is Ezekiel's formula for those who die outside the covenant. In Sheol, that absence is permanent. The NT transposes this: those who die outside Christ bear their own shame; those who die in Christ are covered by his righteousness (2 Corinthians 5:21).
26: A structural note: 'Meshech-Tubal is there and all her multitude, her graves all around it, all of them uncircumcised, slain by the sword; for they spread their terror in the land of the living.' Meshech-Tubal — the distant northern peoples who will reappear in the Gog oracle (Ezekiel 38:2-3) — are already in Sheol. Their Sheol-presence in chapter 32 and their appearance as the invading force in chapters 38-39 creates a canonical irony: the nations that Gog leads are the nations already judged. The final battle is against powers that are, in the deepest sense, already dead.
27: A pattern: 'And they do not lie with the mighty fallen of old who went down to Sheol with their weapons of war, whose swords were laid under their heads and whose shields are upon their bones; for the terror of the mighty was in the land of the living.' The “mighty fallen of old” buried with honor — weapons under their heads, shields as pillows — are distinguished from the dishonored nations. Even in Sheol, honor and dishonor persist. The ultimately decisive category is not warrior-honor but covenant: those who fall in YHWH's covenant are those who rise (Daniel 12:2; 1 Thessalonians 4:16).
28: A structural note: 'But as for you, you shall be broken and lie among the uncircumcised, with those who are slain by the sword.' Pharaoh explicitly placed among the uncircumcised — the supreme irony for a ruler whose land practiced circumcision as a ritual. In covenant terms, Pharaoh is classed with the pagans: not because of ritual but because of relationship. The NT expansion: “For no one is a Jew who is merely one outwardly ... circumcision is a matter of the heart” (Romans 2:28-29). True circumcision is of the heart — and Pharaoh's heart was never circumcised.
29: A structural note: 'Edom is there, her kings and all her princes, who for all their might are laid with those who are slain by the sword; they lie with the uncircumcised, with those who go down to the pit.' Edom — Israel's brother-nation (Genesis 25:23-26) — is in Sheol with the uncircumcised. Despite Edom's kinship with Israel, its persistent hostility to YHWH's people put it in the class of the nations. Spiritual genealogy outweighs biological genealogy: the children of Abraham are those who share Abraham's faith (Galatians 3:7), not merely his bloodline.
30: A structural note: 'The princes of the north are there, all of them, and all the Sidonians, who have gone down in shame with the slain, for all the terror that they caused by their might; they lie uncircumcised with those who are slain by the sword and bear their shame with those who go down to the pit.' North and south, east and west — the whole compass of ancient powers is represented in Sheol. This universality of the nations in Sheol corresponds to the universality of the resurrection from it: “the sea gave up the dead who were in it, Death and Hades gave up the dead who were in them” (Revelation 20:13).
31: A direct revelation: 'When Pharaoh sees them he will be comforted for all his multitude, Pharaoh and all his army, slain by the sword, declares the Lord GOD.' Pharaoh is “comforted” to see all the other great powers in Sheol with him — the misery-loves-company comfort of the condemned. This is the anti-comfort of death: shared ruin rather than shared life. The resurrection-comfort Christ offers is the total inversion: shared life, shared resurrection, shared glory (John 17:22-24; Romans 8:17). The comfort of Sheol diminishes; the comfort of Christ's resurrection is eternal.
32: A direct revelation: 'For I spread my terror in the land of the living; and he shall be laid to rest among the uncircumcised, with those who are slain by the sword, Pharaoh and all his multitude, declares the Lord GOD.' The final word of the Egypt oracle: Pharaoh “spread terror in the land of the living” — his identity was terror-spreading — and he is laid with the uncircumcised dead. The contrast with Christ's final identity could not be greater: Christ spread life in the land of the living (“I came that they may have life and have it abundantly,” John 10:10) and was laid in a tomb from which he rose, now holding “the keys of Death and Hades” (Revelation 1:18). Pharaoh goes down and stays down; Christ goes down and comes back up, making the way for all who are in him to follow.
